---
title: Quick start
guide-section: "Getting started"
---

This walks through building a response, tabulating the risk set, and fitting the
Kaplan-Meier and Nelson-Aalen estimators, all validated against R.

## Build a response

```python
import greenwood as gw
from greenwood import Surv

df = gw.data.load_dataset("lung")

# survival::lung codes status as 1 = censored, 2 = dead, so convert explicitly.
y = Surv.right(df["time"], event=(df["status"] == 2))
y
# Surv(type=right, n=228, events=165)
```

Other censoring flavors:

```python
Surv.counting(start=df["t0"], stop=df["t1"], event=df["ev"])   # left truncation / time-varying
Surv.interval(lower=df["lo"], upper=df["hi"])                  # interval-censored
Surv.multistate(df["time"], event=df["cause"], states=("relapse", "death"))  # competing risks
```

## Tabulate the risk set

`event_table` is the kernel every estimator builds on. It matches R's `survfit` tabulation:

```python
et = gw.event_table(y)
et.to_dataframe().head()
#    time  n_risk  n_event  n_censor
# 0     5     228        1         0
# 1    11     227        3         0
# ...
```

Stratify by a group:

```python
gw.event_table(y, group=df["sex"]).to_dataframe()
```

## Fit Kaplan-Meier

`KaplanMeier` estimates the survival function with Greenwood confidence intervals. Choose
the interval transform with `conf_type` (`"log"`, the R default, `"plain"`, or
`"log-log"`), and stratify with `by`:

```python
km = gw.KaplanMeier(conf_type="log-log").fit(y, by=df["sex"])

km.to_dataframe()      # strata, time, n_risk, n_event, n_censor, estimate, conf_low, conf_high
km.median(ci=True)     # {1: (270.0, 210.0, 306.0), 2: (426.0, 345.0, 524.0)}
km.quantile(0.25)      # first-quartile survival time per stratum
km.rmst(365, ci=True)  # restricted mean survival time up to 365 days, per stratum
km.predict([180, 365]) # survival probability at 180 and 365 days
```

Broom-style summaries are available through the tidy layer, so Great Summaries can build
tables directly on a fitted curve:

```python
gw.tidy.tidy(km)       # one row per time point
gw.tidy.glance(km)     # one row per stratum: events, median, median_lower, median_upper
```

## Nelson-Aalen cumulative hazard

```python
na = gw.NelsonAalen().fit(y)
na.to_dataframe()      # time, n_risk, n_event, estimate (cumulative hazard), std_error, CI
```

## Compare groups

`logrank_test` compares survival across two or more groups. The default is the standard
log-rank test; set `rho`/`gamma` for the G-rho (Fleming-Harrington) family, where
`rho=1, gamma=0` gives the Peto-Peto (Wilcoxon-type) test:

```python
result = gw.logrank_test(y, group=df["sex"])
result.statistic       # 10.3267
result.p_value         # 0.00131
result.df              # 1
result.observed        # weighted observed events per group
result.expected        # weighted expected events per group

gw.logrank_test(y, group=df["sex"], rho=1)   # Peto-Peto weighting
```

## Visualize

`plot_survival` returns a composable plotnine plot with confidence ribbons and censoring
marks. Pass `risk_table=True` to stack an aligned numbers-at-risk table beneath it:

```python
gw.plot_survival(km)                                  # a plotnine ggplot
gw.plot_survival(km, risk_table=True)                 # curve + aligned risk table
gw.plot_survival(km, conf_int=False, censor_marks=False)
```

Because the result is a plotnine object, you can keep composing it with themes, scales, and
labels, and the risk-table times are configurable (`gw.plot_survival(km, times=[0, 200,
400, 600])`).

## Cox proportional hazards

`CoxPH` fits the proportional-hazards model by maximizing the partial likelihood (Efron
ties by default, or `ties="breslow"`). Pass a `Surv` response and a covariate design (a
dataframe or a 2-D array); non-numeric columns are treatment-coded, and rows with missing
covariates are dropped:

```python
cox = gw.CoxPH().fit(y, df[["age", "sex"]])

# Stratified baselines (shared coefficients), and the robust sandwich variance:
gw.CoxPH().fit(y, df[["age", "ph.ecog"]], strata=df["sex"])
gw.CoxPH().fit(y, df[["age", "sex"]], robust=True)
gw.CoxPH().fit(y, df[["age", "sex"]], cluster=df["inst"])   # clustered robust variance

cox.coef_            # log-hazard-ratio coefficients
cox.hazard_ratio_    # exp(coef)
cox.std_error_       # model-based standard errors
cox.p_value_         # Wald z-test p-values
cox.loglik_, cox.lr_stat_, cox.wald_stat_, cox.score_stat_
```

The tidy layer gives broom-style output, so Great Summaries can build a regression table on
top of a fitted model:

```python
gw.tidy.tidy(cox, exponentiate=True)   # term, estimate (HR), std_error, statistic, p_value, CI
gw.tidy.glance(cox)                    # n, nevent, loglik, aic, likelihood-ratio test
```

### Diagnostics and prediction

```python
cox.baseline_hazard()                  # time, cumhaz, survival (uncentered baseline)
cox.predict(type="lp")                 # centered linear predictors
cox.predict(newdata, type="survival", times=[180, 365])  # survival curves for new subjects

cox.residuals("martingale")            # one per observation
cox.residuals("schoenfeld")            # one row per event, per covariate

cox.cox_zph()                          # Grambsch-Therneau proportional-hazards test
cox.concordance()                      # Harrell's C-statistic
```

The proportional-hazards test returns per-covariate and global chi-square tests (with an
`identity` or `log` time transform):

```python
z = cox.cox_zph(transform="identity")
z.to_dataframe()       # term, chisq, df, p_value (rows for each covariate plus GLOBAL)
```

## Parametric AFT models

`AFT` fits accelerated failure time models by maximum likelihood, with coefficients on the
log-time scale (as in R's `survreg`). Choose the error distribution with `dist`:

```python
aft = gw.AFT("weibull").fit(y, df[["age", "sex"]])   # or "exponential", "lognormal", "loglogistic"

aft.coef_          # coefficients (including the intercept)
aft.scale_         # scale parameter (1 for the exponential model)
gw.tidy.tidy(aft)  # tidy coefficient table
gw.tidy.glance(aft)  # dist, n, nevent, scale, loglik, aic
```

## Competing risks

`AalenJohansen` estimates the cumulative incidence function (CIF) for each competing cause.
Build a multi-state response where the event codes are 0 for censoring and 1, 2, ... for
the causes:

```python
mg = gw.data.load_dataset("mgus2")
etime = mg["ptime"].where(mg["pstat"] == 1, mg["futime"])
cause = mg["pstat"].where(mg["pstat"] == 1, 2 * mg["death"])   # 0 censor, 1 pcm, 2 death
y = Surv.multistate(etime, event=cause, states=("pcm", "death"))

aj = gw.AalenJohansen().fit(y, by=mg["sex"])
aj.to_dataframe()   # strata, cause, time, n_risk, estimate (CIF), std_error, conf_low, conf_high
```

The `FineGray` subdistribution hazard model regresses covariates on the cumulative
incidence of a target cause, with clustered robust standard errors:

```python
fg = gw.FineGray("pcm").fit(y, mg[["age", "sex"]])
gw.tidy.tidy(fg, exponentiate=True)   # subdistribution hazard ratios
fg.std_error_                         # clustered robust standard errors
```

For general multi-state models, `MultiState` estimates the Aalen-Johansen transition and
state-occupancy probabilities from counting-process intervals (each labelled with the state
occupied and the state transitioned to):

```python
ms = gw.MultiState().fit(
    start=intervals["t0"], stop=intervals["t1"],
    state=intervals["from_state"], event=intervals["to_state"],
)
ms.to_dataframe()      # occupancy probability over time, one column per state
ms.predict([12, 60])   # occupancy at specific times
ms.transition_         # the P(0, t) transition-probability matrices
```

## Prediction performance

Evaluate a fitted model or an external risk score with the concordance index and the
inverse-probability-of-censoring-weighted (IPCW) Brier score:

```python
cox = gw.CoxPH().fit(y, df[["age", "sex"]])

gw.concordance_index(y, cox.predict(type="lp"))   # Harrell's C

times = [180, 365, 540]
surv = cox.predict(df[["age", "sex"]], type="survival", times=times)
probs = surv.iloc[:, 1:].to_numpy().T             # (n_obs, len(times))
gw.brier_score(y, probs, times)                   # IPCW Brier score at each time
gw.integrated_brier_score(y, probs, times)         # time-averaged Brier score
```

## Round-trip the response

The `Surv` object serializes deterministically, the basis for reproducible pipelines:

```python
text = y.to_json()
restored = Surv.from_json(text)
assert restored.to_json() == text
```
